title: Balancing minority and majority rights
description: Explain how the Court has at times allowed the restriction of the civil rights of minority groups and at other times has protected those rights.
topic_path: How does the government balance majority rule and minority rights?

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The District Court’s assessment that all this evidence proved racial predominance clears the bar of clear error review. The court emphasized that the districting plan’s own architects had repeatedly described the influx of African-Americans into District 12 as a . . . compliance measure, not a side-effect of political gerrymandering. And those contemporaneous descriptions comported with the court’s credibility determinations about the trial testimony—that Watt told the truth when he recounted Rucho’s resolve to hit a majority-BVAP target; and conversely that Hofeller skirted the truth (especially as to Guilford County) when he claimed to have followed only race-blind criteria in drawing district lines. We cannot disrespect such credibility judgments.”

> -Associate Justice Elena Kagan, opinion of the Court in *Cooper v. Harris,* 2017

**Which of the following statements best summarizes the author’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: North Carolina created a district predominantly based on racial lines, which is unconstitutional
North Carolina created districts predominantly based on race in accordance with the Voting Rights Act, making it a constitutional action
North Carolina created a districts predominantly based on partisan lines, which is unconstitutional
North Carolina created districts predominantly based on population changes, making it a constitutional action

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The District Court’s assessment that all this evidence proved racial predominance clears the bar of clear error review. The court emphasized that the districting plan’s own architects had repeatedly described the influx of African-Americans into District 12 as a . . . compliance measure, not a side-effect of political gerrymandering. And those contemporaneous descriptions comported with the court’s credibility determinations about the trial testimony—that Watt told the truth when he recounted Rucho’s resolve to hit a majority-BVAP target; and conversely that Hofeller skirted the truth (especially as to Guilford County) when he claimed to have followed only race-blind criteria in drawing district lines. We cannot disrespect such credibility judgments.”

> -Associate Justice Elena Kagan, opinion of the Court in *Cooper v. Harris,* 2017

**Based on the text above, which of the following statements would the author agree with? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Majority-minority districts are unconstitutional when those districts are created based on racial differences
Majority-minority districts are constitutional when the state is trying to meet standards set by the Voting Rights Act of 1965
Majority-minority districts are unconstitutional when there is a clear partisan divide as well as a racial divide
Majority-minority districts are only constitutional when there are not enough minority representatives in state legislatures

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The District Court’s assessment that all this evidence proved racial predominance clears the bar of clear error review. The court emphasized that the districting plan’s own architects had repeatedly described the influx of African-Americans into District 12 as a . . . compliance measure, not a side-effect of political gerrymandering. And those contemporaneous descriptions comported with the court’s credibility determinations about the trial testimony—that Watt told the truth when he recounted Rucho’s resolve to hit a majority-BVAP target; and conversely that Hofeller skirted the truth (especially as to Guilford County) when he claimed to have followed only race-blind criteria in drawing district lines. We cannot disrespect such credibility judgments.”

> -Associate Justice Elena Kagan, opinion of the Court in *Cooper v. Harris,* 2017

**Supporters of Kagan’s view that a state cannot use race as a predominant factor in creating districts could cite which of the following cases as precedent?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Shaw v. Reno* (1993)
*Baker v. Carr* (1961)
*McCulloch v. Maryland* (1819)
*Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission* (2010)

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/834bf9ff8130e596d80192ec24ee8361.png)

>Source: [Wikimedia Commons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shaw_v._Reno#/media/File:United_States_Congressional_Districts_in_North_Carolina,_1993_%E2%80%93_1998.tif)

In 1993, the Supreme Court struck down the formation of North Carolina’s 12th congressional district (shown in pink) in *Shaw v. Reno* (1993). 

**Which of the following best explains why the court ruled against North Carolina?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: North Carolina formed an oddly-shaped district that was a majority-minority district
North Carolina created too many legislative districts and was only allowed to make 11 districts
North Carolina did not have the majority-minority district that is required under the Voting Rights Act of 1965
North Carolina obviously gerrymandered their districts to benefit representatives of one political party over the other

assessment_items.question_content:
**Based on previous rulings, which of the following scenarios would the Supreme Court most likely rule violate the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The state of Illinois creates an all-black  congressional district
A judge refuses to provide a defendant legal counsel
A lawyer uses evidence found without a warrant to convict a suspect
The state of Montana passes a law banning all abortions

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is an accurate comparison of the two court cases?**

 | *Brown v. Board of Education* (1954) | *Shaw v. Reno* (1993)
- | - | -
**A** | Ruled that Kansas violated the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment | Ruled that North Carolina violated the due process clause of the Fourteenth Amendment
**B** | Prohibited race-based segregation in schools | Prohibited oddly-shaped majority-minority districts
**C** | Decided that states had the exclusive right to regulate schools | Decided that federal courts can intervene and decide redistricting cases
**D** | Led to the “one-person, one-vote” judicial doctrine | Led to an increase in power for the national government

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Comparison A
Comparison B
Comparison C
Comparison D

assessment_items.question_content:
In 1890, Louisiana enacted a law that required separate railway cars for black people and for white people.  Homer Plessy took a seat in a “whites only” train. He refused to move to the car reserved for black people and was arrested. The case went to the Supreme Court and the Supreme Court ruled to uphold the Louisiana law. 

**Which statement accurately summarizes the impact of the *Plessy v. Ferguson* (1896) decision? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It restricted African-American access to the same public facilities as the majority white population
It restricted African-American voting power by ruling that poll taxes and literacy tests were constitutional
It protected African-American access to the same public facilities as the majority white population
It protected African-American voting power by requiring that states create majority-minority legislative districts

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following Supreme Court Cases ruled that “separate but equal” public schools are a violation of the equal protection clause of the 14th Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Brown v. Board of Education* (1954)
*Shaw v. Reno* (1993)
*Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District* (1969)
*Gideon v. Wainwright* (1963)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement accurately summarizes the impact of the *Brown v. Board of Education* (1954) decision?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It protected African-American rights to attend any public school and ruled that race-based school segregation unconstitutional
It protected African-American access to the same public facilities as the majority white population
It restricted African-American access to the same public facilities as the majority white population
It restricted African-American voting power by deciding that poll taxes and literacy tests were constitutional

assessment_items.question_content:
In 1890, Louisiana enacted a law that required separate railway cars for black people and for white people.  Homer Plessy took a seat in a “whites only” train. He refused to move to the car reserved for black people and was arrested. In *Plessy v. Ferguson* (1896),  the Supreme Court ruled in a 7-1 vote to uphold the Louisiana law.

**Which of the following constitutional clauses does this case have in common with *Brown v. Board of Education* (1954)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The equal protection clause
The due process clause
The establishment clause
The free exercise clause

assessment_items.question_content:
In *Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenberg County Schools* (1971), the Supreme Court held that schools could assign students to schools outside of their district to achieve racial balances. 

**Which of the following cases did the Court use as precedent in their decision?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Brown v. Board of Education* (1954)
*Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District* (1969)
*Engel v. Vitale* (1962)
*Gideon v. Wainwright* (1963)

assessment_items.question_content:
In *Heart of Atlanta Motel v. United States* (1964), the Supreme Court ruled that the federal government has the right to regulate public accommodation under the commerce clause. After this decision, the federal government, under the authority of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, forced public places to desegregate.

**This scenario is an example of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Supreme Court issuing a ruling that protects minority rights
The Supreme Court issuing a ruling that restricts majority rights
The Supreme Court issuing a ruling that prohibits majority-minority districts
The Supreme Court issuing a ruling that upholds the “separate but equal” doctrine
